concerns.text: FR-006 mandates 'out-of-sample held-out recession periods' for validation. The idea and User Story 3 only specify 'bootstrapping' and 'sensitivity analysis' on interpolation thresholds. While robust, holding out specific recession periods (e.g., 2008 or 2020) as a test set is a distinct methodological choice not explicitly requested in the idea, potentially reducing the training data available for the Granger causality test on the remaining periods. This is an over-reach on the validation strategy.

SC-006 references '5–[deferred] of data' for sensitivity analysis. The spec text in US-3 mentions '5–[deferred] of data is randomly masked', but the Success Criteria phrasing 'reported shifts in p-values not exceeding a defined threshold (e.g., 0.01)' introduces a specific pass/fail threshold (0.01) for the shift magnitude. The idea did not specify a tolerance for p-value drift; setting a hard threshold for 'success' redefines the research question from 'does sentiment predict economy?' to 'does the model remain stable under 0.01 p-value drift?', which is a scope drift.

FR-010 mandates excluding periods if low-confidence sentiment data exceeds '10% of the dataset'. The idea and US-1 only specify flagging low-confidence periods and ensuring the missing rate is ≤5% for interpolation. The 10% exclusion threshold is an arbitrary engineering constraint not derived from the research question. If the dataset has 11% low-confidence weeks, the spec forces exclusion of the entire dataset or a major scope change, whereas the idea only asked for handling missing data.
